text: Alonso’s comment about Michael Schumacher having kids when he overtook him on the outside of 130R. He did say it, but it was about 2 weeks after the race, and the meaning of it is I think often misunderstood/misrepresented. Firstly I think he was only being semi-serious when he said it. Secondly, I don’t think it was intended to be a demonstration of being some sort of ruthless, fearless, “ice in the veins” racer. It was more about how he could try a move like that because he could trust Schumacher not to do anything stupid.
post_title: What's your "Formula 1 Mandela Effect"?

text: I don’t think it was intended to be a demonstration of being some sort of ruthless, fearless, “ice in the veins” racer Yeah its weird that this meaning has stuck around forever when it is super out of character for Fernando lol. If you see any footage of him talking, aside from the times where he is pissed at the car, he's always at least half joking. He isn't a super serious dude trying to drop one liners like that. The real line is half joke, half a bit of reflection about how drivers used to change the way they drove as they got older and responsible for more lives when F1 was still quite dangerous. If anything, it was more of a memento mori moment than an "I'm so fearless because racing is all I have" moment.

text: Nobody uses Mandela Effect properly. It's a phenomenon where a large group of people "remember" a particular event/thing that didn't actually happen. Instead people just say it anytime they themselves misremember something.

text: Endurance racing is a little strange in that regard with so many series and relatively small calendars for each one, a lot of drivers race in multiple series and often have to choose which to show up to. The top drivers are in Fuji for the WEC race, so that takes priority, and a lot of others only use NLS as warm up + practice for the Nurburgring 24 hours. Max did really well, but without the likes of Falken, Manthey, ROWE and the other top GT3 teams competing it's hard to say how well he'd do in a 24 hour race. All we can say is that his laps were rapid and consistent, and his traffic management was very clean. He's definitely got a shot at the 24 next year.

text: No problem - and I forgot to answer your first question, sorry! The SP9 field was very small compared to normal with only 9 others to compete with, and with a mixed up grid from the rain the only real competition was the pole sitting Aston which picked up aero damage at turn 1. The Mustangs were able to keep close to Max's pace but their weak starting positions made it much harder for them to catch up. The 2025 Nurburgring 24 hour race, for context, had 27 cars in SP9 with 134 total entries. Far more competitive and much more traffic to fight through.

text: I'll explain it a bit more because I'm not saying the tweet from Vincent Bruins was incorrect. He tweeted that during the race and was likely looking at the live timing and scoring which shows the last time set by all the drivers next to the fastest lap from the whole race. Ignoring the fastest lap column, the lap time column only tells you how fast everyone is going at that specific point in the race. So the lap when Lance set the 1:55.796 very likely was the fastest lap set from anyone on that same lap, but that's only 1 lap, it doesn't mean it was faster than everyone else for all the other laps before and after that moment while Lance was in the car.

text: Just had to check this, and yes, Williams currently hold "longest gap between wins of any current team that has previously won". Alpine - last won in 2021. Four seasons since last win. Aston Martin - never won, but if you count Racing Point as the same team, last won in 2020. Five seasons since last win. Ferrari - last won in 2024. One season since last win. McLaren - last won in 2025. No seasons since last win. Mercedes - last won in 2025. No seasons since last win. Racing Bulls - never won as RB but if you count Toro Rosso then Alpha Tarui as the same team, last won in 2020. Five seasons since last win. Red Bull - last won in 2025. No seasons since last win. Williams - last won in 2012. 13 seasons since last win. Audi, Cadillac, and HAAS have never won. Though if you count Audi as the same team as Sauber, they have had 17 seasons without a win, last winning as BMW Sauber in 2008. EDIT - to add too. If Aston Martin win at any point, they'll take the record for "longest time between first entry and first win." Their first entry in F1 was the 1959 Dutch Grand Prix. They competed in two dreadful seasons before pulling out at the end of 1960. If they win in 2026 it will be 67 years between their first entry and first win.
